title: Grades posted · final lessons up · final exam coming
body:
I've graded your tests and posted grades to date on Blackboard. If you don't see a grade for an assignment or test, it means I don't have a submission from you. This happens for multiple reasons — broken links instead of attached files, wrong file format, etc. If you see something that looks off, let me know ASAP.

I've also posted slides and lectures for the final two lessons on confidence intervals and regression analysis. I'm going to post some more videos that run through examples in the next couple of days, and post the final toward the end of the week. Same format as before — one test in Blackboard (multiple choice, timed) and the modeling piece will be untimed so you can work through at your own pace. Look for that starting late Thursday/Friday.

Grades were really good on the modeling and the online pieces last week, so great work everyone! If you're not happy with your grade, or if you want to see me work through some of the problems, I've posted videos to the course site. Feel free to resubmit the Excel piece if you want to boost your grade — I'll award 1/2 credit on follow-up submissions.

Thank you all for bearing with me this term; I appreciate your flexibility as I continue to gear this course toward more relevant skills.

I'll be in touch if you have any questions.

—Holloway

title: Exam 1 posted — due Friday 5/8 by 11:59 PM
body:
Exam 1 is up. Download "Modeling Problems" from the Resources page (or the Lessons page under Week 7).

Due: Friday, May 8 at 11:59 PM.

Three modeling problems in one workbook:
1) Bollinger Bands — build a 20-day moving average ±1σ and ±2σ on Tesla daily prices, then chart it.
2) Sector Allocation — work through the S&P 500 list and decide how to allocate 100 portfolio positions across the 11 GICS sectors based on cost, time, and rigor.
3) Pro Forma — build a simplified P&G income statement for FY26 using the historical data and the scenario probabilities.

Each problem has its own instructions tab with step-by-step guidance and the questions you need to answer.

Open notes, open everything. You can use any tool, any AI, any source. The goal is to produce a working model — show your work in the spreadsheet itself (formulas, not pasted values).

If anything in the workbook looks broken or unclear, post in the discussion or email me — don't guess.

title: Welcome — start with probability
body:
Welcome, class. Take a look around the site and let me know if anything looks off or you have comments.

Go ahead and start working through the probability lecture up on the Lessons page — that's Week 4, Chapter 5. I'll drop some supplemental exercises alongside it shortly.

Stay tuned.
